summary: Reynoldstown finally sees development breaking ground after years of delays while ICE agents deploy to Hartsfield-Jackson amid massive TSA lines. Decatur's getting village-style housing and a major Belgian biotech company just placed a huge bet on Georgia.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near Beltline Finally Breaks Ground After Five Years
topStory.summary: That cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing some action. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. The site has been sitting empty since Toll Brothers cleared it nearly five years ago.
topStory.body:
If you've been walking the Beltline wondering what was going to happen with that big empty lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace, your wait is almost over. After sitting vacant for nearly five years, the 2-acre site in Reynoldstown is finally seeing groundwork begin for what will become 85 townhomes and condos.

Embry Development picked up the project from Toll Brothers two years ago and is moving forward with the development they're calling Camber Crossing. The timing feels right with the Eastside Trail just a block away continuing to draw people to the area. You know that stretch between Memorial Drive and the Freedom Park Trail - it's been one of the last big gaps waiting to be filled in along the corridor.

This is exactly the kind of infill development that makes sense for Reynoldstown. The neighborhood has seen steady growth around the Beltline, but unlike some areas, it hasn't been completely overwhelmed. Having more housing options within walking distance of the trail should help meet demand without feeling like it's changing the character of what's already there.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site showing groundwork beginning for Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

stories.imageAlt: Construction site showing groundwork beginning for Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Gets Village-Style Community
stories.summary: Construction is underway for Cottages at Midway, bringing 18 standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. It's designed to be more affordable than the area's typical $700,000+ homes while maintaining that single-family feel everyone wants.
stories.body:
With median home prices in Decatur pushing past $700,000, finding something affordable and still ITP feels nearly impossible. That's exactly what makes Cottages at Midway interesting. This 2.1-acre development on Midway Road is trying to thread the needle between affordability and the kind of housing people actually want to live in.

The 18 houses are arranged around shared courtyards and green space, giving it more of a village feel than your typical subdivision. It's replacing a couple of underused lots on a residential street, which means it fits into the existing neighborhood fabric instead of feeling dropped in from somewhere else. The soft launch starts next week, so you'll be able to get a better sense of what they're actually asking for these places.

This is exactly the kind of 'missing middle' housing that planners love to talk about but developers rarely build. It's denser than single-family homes but not as dense as apartments, and it preserves some of that community feeling that makes neighborhoods like Decatur appealing in the first place.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of Cottages at Midway development with houses arranged around courtyards

stories.headline: West End Townhomes Along Beltline Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is starting to rise along the Westside Trail. Ackerman & Co. is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to West End, with the first seven units hitting the market this spring.
stories.body:
The Westside Trail has been waiting for more housing options, and GW Parkside is starting to deliver. The 23-townhome development at 600 Hopkins Street is finally showing some real progress after Ackerman & Co. started demolition a year ago. If you've been walking that stretch of the Beltline, you've probably been watching the site wondering when something would actually happen.

What makes this interesting is that for-sale townhomes are pretty rare along the Westside Trail compared to what you see on the east side. Most of the residential development over here has been apartments and condos. Having more ownership options should help the area feel more settled and give people a reason to stick around long-term.

Ackerman knows what they're doing - they also own Lee + White with all its food and retail options just down the trail. Having the same developer behind both projects should mean they'll complement each other well instead of competing.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering showing townhomes under construction for GW Parkside development in West End

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Atlanta Airport as TSA Lines Hit Breaking Point
stories.summary: Immigration agents are now walking around Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security as TSA agents work without pay during the government shutdown. Airport officials warned of four-hour wait times Monday, and travelers report the ICE agents aren't doing much beyond providing a federal presence.
stories.body:
The situation at Hartsfield-Jackson keeps getting worse, and now we have ICE agents walking around the terminals trying to help manage the chaos. Mayor Andre Dickens announced the deployment Sunday, emphasizing that the agents won't be doing immigration enforcement - they're just there to assist with crowd control and security as TSA agents continue working without paychecks.

Travelers report mixed results. Some say wait times dropped from the catastrophic four-hour estimates to more manageable 30-minute waits by Tuesday, but others say the ICE agents are mostly just standing around. The real problem is that nearly half of TSA agents have been calling out of work, and you can't really blame them for not showing up when they haven't been paid in over a month.

This is exactly the kind of situation that makes you appreciate how much infrastructure we take for granted. The world's busiest airport grinding to a halt because of a political fight in Washington affects everyone who flies through Atlanta, which is pretty much everyone.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear walking through Atlanta airport terminal

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Avenue Gets Its Complete Streets Makeover
stories.summary: After nearly a decade in planning, Decatur's bike lane project on West Howard Avenue is almost done. The one-mile stretch now has a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue was always more of a drag strip than a neighborhood street, but Decatur's Complete Streets project is finally changing that. The nearly finished bike lane runs from Commerce Drive near Decatur High all the way to the city limits at East Lake MARTA station. If you've been avoiding that stretch because of fast-moving traffic, the concrete-barrier protected cycle track should make it much more comfortable.

This has been in the works since 2017, which shows you how long these infrastructure projects take even when everyone agrees they're a good idea. The timing works well with all the development happening around East Lake and gives people a safer way to bike between the neighborhoods and downtown Decatur.

The real test will be whether drivers actually slow down. Decatur did more than just add bike lanes - they also narrowed travel lanes and added other traffic calming measures. It's one thing to paint some lines on the street, but this looks like a more serious commitment to making the road work for everyone instead of just cars.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect Peking chicken platters, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis in a space that feels like a neighborhood gem.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a serious new restaurant from two chefs who know what they're doing. Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened Mushi Ni today on Elizabeth Street, and it's the kind of place the neighborhood has been missing. Le worked at some of Atlanta's best restaurants while Jimenez comes from Gordon Ramsay's kitchens in New York, so they're bringing real experience to their first venture.

The menu hits that sweet spot of Asian-American comfort food done right. The Peking chicken platter feeds two people for $25, which feels like a steal for this caliber of cooking. They're also doing interesting bao like soft shell crab and chicken longanisa, plus cocktails that sound actually thoughtful instead of afterthoughts.

The location works perfectly - right off the Freedom Park Trail where you can grab dinner after a walk or bike ride. Elizabeth Street has been slowly developing more restaurants and shops, and this feels like the kind of place that makes the whole strip stronger.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish served at Mushi Ni restaurant in Inman Park

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens Near East Lake in April
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening Vela Park Food Works in mid-April. The eastside food hall will feature six chef-driven concepts with outdoor dining at the new Vela Park apartment community near East Lake Golf Club.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to do food halls right. The guy behind New York's famous Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta concept to the eastside with Vela Park Food Works, opening in mid-April at the new Vela Park apartment development near East Lake Golf Club.

This one sounds more polished than your typical food court. Six different chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and plenty of outdoor dining space should make it feel more like a destination than just a place to grab lunch. The timing works well with all the development happening around East Lake - people need places to eat, and this gives them options beyond the usual chain restaurants.

Montwaid's other Atlanta food halls have done well, so he clearly understands the market here. The eastside has been waiting for something like this, especially with the Beltline bringing more foot traffic to the area. Having it connected to a new apartment complex means there's built-in customer base, but the real test will be whether it draws people from other neighborhoods too.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining area and food stalls

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Texas-Style Smokehouse to the Beltline
stories.summary: The Austin pitmaster just opened at Ansley Mall with six massive smokers running 24/7 in a glass-walled loft overlooking the new Beltline bridge. John Lewis is serving some of the best brisket in Atlanta from tanks he helped weld himself.
stories.body:
The new Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall is unlike any smokehouse you've seen in Atlanta. John Lewis put six 22-foot smokers in a glass-walled loft with panoramic views of the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks more like the fanciest engine room in Georgia than a typical barbecue joint, and the brisket coming out of those custom-welded smokers is absolutely worth the spectacle.

Lewis helped build those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they run 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts. That's the kind of commitment that separates real barbecue from the pretenders. The guy knows what he's doing - he's from Austin by way of Charleston, so he understands both Texas-style brisket and how to make it work for Southern palates.

The location makes perfect sense. Ansley Mall has been transforming into more of a food destination, and having a serious barbecue spot right on the Beltline means you can grab lunch after a walk or bike ride. The timing couldn't be better with all the foot traffic the area gets now.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue with brisket and sides

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Firefighters Push Back
stories.summary: Atlanta's Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities and require documentation of agent activities. Meanwhile, dozens of firefighters showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for not signing their first-ever union contract.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is moving forward on two fronts to limit ICE operations in the city, even as federal agents are currently deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document whenever federal immigration agents are present during their operations.

The timing feels significant with ICE agents currently walking around the airport, though city officials emphasize those agents are only helping with security during the TSA shortage. The full council will vote on both resolutions April 20, and they're expected to pass given the committee's unanimous support.

But the bigger drama at the meeting was about firefighters. Dozens showed up to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for refusing to sign the union contract that city council ratified nearly a year ago. It's the first contract in the fire department's history, and the firefighters are clearly frustrated that it's been sitting unsigned for so long while they continue working under old terms.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta firefighters speaking at city council meeting

stories.headline: Decatur School Board Plans Community Meeting on Budget Controversy
stories.summary: City Schools of Decatur will host a community meeting March 25 to address questions about the upcoming budget, the controversial Early Childhood Learning Center, and the Board Management Oversight Plan. Tensions are high over cost and transparency concerns.
stories.body:
There's a lot of frustration in Decatur right now over school district finances, and the school board is trying to address it with a community meeting tomorrow night. The March 25 meeting will cover the upcoming budget, the controversial Early Childhood Education Center that's eating up a huge chunk of funding, and the Board Management Oversight Plan that's supposed to provide more accountability.

The Early Childhood Learning Center has become a lightning rod for concerns about cost and process. People are asking hard questions about whether the district really needs such an expensive facility and whether the community had enough input into the decision. The fact that the board felt the need to schedule a special meeting shows how much pushback they're getting.

Decatur schools have a reputation for excellence, but that comes with high expectations for fiscal responsibility and transparency. When parents start questioning whether their tax dollars are being spent wisely, school board meetings get uncomfortable fast. Tomorrow's meeting will be a good test of whether the district can rebuild trust with the community.

stories.headline: Climate Leaders Target Heat Islands in Atlanta's Black Neighborhoods
stories.summary: UrbanHeatATL and other groups are working to address extreme heat in underserved communities during the annual Parks and Greenspace Conference. Atlanta's tree canopy helps overall, but heat islands disproportionately affect Black neighborhoods with less green space.
stories.body:
Atlanta calls itself 'the city in a forest,' but that nickname doesn't mean much if you live in neighborhoods without trees. At Monday's Parks and Greenspace Conference at the Atlanta Botanical Garden, climate justice leaders talked about how heat islands disproportionately affect Black communities in metro Atlanta, even as other parts of the city stay relatively cool.

UrbanHeatATL is encouraging residents in these communities to build coalitions and engage with property management companies to push for more green infrastructure. It's not just about comfort - extreme heat is the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the U.S., and urban heat islands can make temperatures significantly higher than nearby areas with more trees and green space.

The conference brought together hundreds of people working on parks and greenspace issues across the Southeast. The focus on resilient parks and communities feels especially relevant as Atlanta prepares to host World Cup events this summer. All that investment in downtown infrastructure is great, but it won't mean much if we can't address climate challenges in neighborhoods that need the most help.
stories.imageAlt: Community members discussing urban heat issues at Parks and Greenspace Conference

stories.headline: Belgian Pharma Giant UCB Bets $2 Billion on Georgia Biotech
stories.summary: UCB selected the massive Rowen development in Dacula for its new U.S. biologics manufacturing campus. The Brussels-based company calls it Gwinnett County's largest capital investment in history, eventually bringing hundreds of high-wage jobs to the 2,000-acre research community.
stories.body:
This is exactly the kind of economic development win Georgia has been chasing for years. UCB, the Brussels-based biopharmaceutical company, just announced it's putting $2 billion into a new U.S. biologics manufacturing campus at Rowen, the planned research and education community in Dacula. Officials are calling it the largest capital investment in Gwinnett County history.

Rowen has been marketing itself as a mini city focused on research and education, and landing UCB as its first major tenant validates that whole concept. The 2,000-acre development off Highway 316 is designed to be more than just an industrial park - it's supposed to create an ecosystem where companies, universities, and research institutions can collaborate.

UCB has been in Georgia for more than three decades, so they know the state's business climate and workforce. The fact that they're doubling down with such a massive investment says something about Georgia's position in the life sciences industry. The hundreds of high-wage jobs this will eventually create should help cement Gwinnett County's transformation from suburban bedroom community to major economic center.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of UCB's planned biologics manufacturing campus at Rowen development
